symptom: Turn signal blinking very fast
beginner_steps: Turn on your hazard lights and walk around the car to find which bulb is dead. Access the bulb from inside the trunk or under the hood to replace it.
mechanic_advice: If replacing the bulb doesn't fix the fast blinking, a mechanic needs to check the wiring.
safety_warning: Ensure the car is completely turned off before touching any electrical bulbs.

symptom: Brake lights don't come on when pressing pedal
possible_fault: Faulty brake light switch or blown fuse
beginner_steps: Check the fuse box for a blown brake light fuse. Visually inspect the brake bulbs in the back to see if they look burnt out.
mechanic_advice: If fuses and bulbs are fine, a mechanic must replace the brake switch under the pedal.
safety_warning: Do not drive without working brake lights as it is highly dangerous.

symptom: Low oil pressure warning light stays on
beginner_steps: Park on flat ground. Pull out the engine oil dipstick, wipe it clean, put it fully back in, and pull it out again. Check if the oil line is below the minimum mark.
mechanic_advice: If the oil is full but the light is still on, DO NOT DRIVE. Call a mechanic immediately.
safety_warning: The engine and oil can be extremely hot. Be careful not to burn yourself.

symptom: Car bounces a lot over speed bumps
beginner_steps: Push down hard on one corner of the car and let go. If the car bounces up and down more than twice, the shocks are likely worn.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic needs to hoist the car and replace the suspension struts safely.
safety_warning: Do not attempt to remove suspension parts yourself; they are under extreme spring pressure.

symptom: Window stuck halfway down
beginner_steps: Try pressing the switch firmly while keeping the car door open. Check if the child window lock button is accidentally pressed down.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic needs to remove the interior door panel to replace the burned-out motor.
safety_warning: Keep fingers clear of the window track while testing the switch.

symptom: Very loud rumbling noise from under the car
beginner_steps: Look underneath the side of the car without crawling under it. Check for a hanging exhaust pipe or visible black soot near pipe joints.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic must weld the exhaust or replace the broken pipe section on a lift.
safety_warning: Exhaust pipes get incredibly hot. Never touch them or crawl under an unsupported vehicle.

symptom: Musty smell when AC is turned on
beginner_steps: Open the glovebox. The cabin filter is usually right behind it. Pull out the old filter and see if it is clogged with leaves or dark dirt. Replace with a new one.
mechanic_advice: If replacing the filter doesn't remove the smell, a mechanic can sanitize the internal AC system.
safety_warning: Wear a mask if you have severe dust allergies when removing a dirty filter.

symptom: Streaks left on windshield in rain
beginner_steps: Lift the wiper arm and inspect the rubber edge. If it is cracked, torn, or feels hard like plastic, it needs replacing. Unclip the old one and slide a new one on.
mechanic_advice: If new blades still leave streaks, a mechanic may need to adjust the wiper arm spring tension.
safety_warning: Don't let a bare metal wiper arm snap back onto the glass; it can crack the windshield.

symptom: Steering wheel vibrates at highway speeds
beginner_steps: Visually check if the tyres have any large chunks of mud stuck to the rims. Check the tyre pressure with a gauge to ensure they are even.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic or tyre shop needs to put the wheels on a balancing machine.
safety_warning: Only pull over in safe locations away from traffic to check your wheels.

symptom: Bike cranks but won't start
beginner_steps: Locate the spark plug on the side of the engine. Pull the thick wire off and check if the plug looks wet or covered in black soot. Clean it with a dry cloth.
mechanic_advice: If a cleaned or new spark plug doesn't work, a mechanic should check the ignition coil.
safety_warning: Ensure the engine is completely cool before touching the spark plug to avoid burns.

symptom: Clanking noise from rear wheel when rolling
beginner_steps: Inspect the chain by pushing it up and down with your finger. If it moves up and down more than an inch, it needs tightening.
mechanic_advice: If the chain is rusted solid or has tight spots, have a mechanic replace the chain and sprockets.
safety_warning: Never put your fingers near the chain or sprockets while the wheel is spinning.

symptom: Squeaking noise when braking
beginner_steps: Look into the brake caliper to see the pad thickness. If the pads look very thin, they need replacing. If they look thick but dirty, wash the area with water.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic must replace the brake pads immediately if they are worn down to the metal backing.
safety_warning: Always test your brakes at a very slow walking speed after cleaning or inspecting them.

symptom: Bike feels heavy to steer
beginner_steps: Check the front tyre with a pressure gauge. Inflate it to the recommended pressure printed on the side of the tyre or in your manual.
mechanic_advice: If the tyre loses air again within a day, a mechanic needs to check for a slow puncture.
safety_warning: Riding on a highly deflated front tyre can cause you to lose control of the bike.

symptom: Headlight is very dim
possible_fault: Weak battery or loose ground wire
beginner_steps: Check if the battery terminals under the seat are screwed down tightly. Inspect the wiring behind the headlight bulb for loose connections.
mechanic_advice: If the wires are tight and bulb is good, a mechanic should test the bike's charging stator.
safety_warning: Do not look directly into the headlight while testing high beams.

symptom: Scooter feels wobbly from the back
beginner_steps: Visually inspect the rear tyre for nails, screws, or flat spots. Do not pull a nail out if you find one, as it will lose air faster.
mechanic_advice: If punctured, a tyre shop or mechanic needs to plug the hole or replace the tyre.
safety_warning: Do not ride on a completely flat tyre as it will destroy the metal wheel rim.

symptom: Electric start makes clicking sound but horn is weak
beginner_steps: Open the battery compartment (usually under the floorboard or seat). Check if the red and black wires are screwed on tightly and are free of white powder.
mechanic_advice: If the wires are clean and tight but it still won't start, a mechanic should test battery health.
safety_warning: Do not let a metal tool touch both the positive and negative battery terminals at the same time.

symptom: Scooter struggles to accelerate
beginner_steps: Locate the black plastic air filter box near the rear wheel. Unscrew the cover and check if the paper filter is choked with dust. Tap it clean or replace it.
mechanic_advice: If the filter is clean but acceleration is still poor, a mechanic needs to clean the carburetor or fuel injector.
safety_warning: Make sure the rubber seal is seated properly when closing the air filter box.

symptom: Brake lever touches the handlebar
beginner_steps: Locate the brake adjustment nut near the rear wheel. Turn it clockwise a few times by hand or with a spanner to tighten the brake lever feel.
mechanic_advice: If the nut is fully tightened but the brake is still weak, a mechanic must install new brake shoes.
safety_warning: Do not over-tighten the brake nut; the wheel should spin freely when the lever is released.

symptom: Reduced driving range and slow acceleration
beginner_steps: Check the dashboard display for the current battery health percentage or warning turtle icon. Ensure the car is not in a restricted Eco mode.
mechanic_advice: A certified EV technician must perform a battery health diagnostic and cell balancing.
safety_warning: NEVER touch orange high-voltage cables. They carry lethal amounts of electricity.

symptom: Charger won't lock into the car port
possible_fault: Debris in port or faulty locking pin
beginner_steps: Shine a light into the charging port to check for leaves, dirt, or ice. Gently blow it out if dirty.
mechanic_advice: If the port is clean but won't lock, a mechanic must replace the charge port actuator.
safety_warning: Do not stick metal objects into the charging port to clean it.

symptom: Car doesn't slow down when lifting off the accelerator
possible_fault: Regen system disabled or fully charged battery
beginner_steps: Check if the battery is at 100% (regen turns off when full). Check the infotainment screen to ensure regen braking wasn't accidentally turned off in settings.
mechanic_advice: If the battery is low and settings are correct, a mechanic must scan the brake control module.
safety_warning: Rely on your physical brake pedal until the system is checked.

symptom: Blind spot light stays on constantly
beginner_steps: Locate the blind spot sensors (usually behind the rear bumper corners) and wipe the bumper clean of mud, snow, or heavy dirt.
mechanic_advice: If cleaning doesn't work, the sensor may be internally damaged or misaligned and requires professional calibration.
safety_warning: Do not rely on broken ADAS systems for safe lane changes.

symptom: Backup camera shows a black or blurry screen
beginner_steps: Walk to the rear of the car and wipe the small camera lens above the license plate.
mechanic_advice: If the lens is clean but the screen is black, a mechanic needs to check the wiring harness in the trunk.
safety_warning: Always turn around and use your mirrors when reversing if the camera is down.

symptom: Radio and touchscreen freeze or go blank
possible_fault: Software glitch or blown interior fuse
beginner_steps: Hold down the power button on the radio for 10 seconds to force a system reboot (works on most modern cars).
mechanic_advice: If rebooting fails, a mechanic may need to flash a software update or replace the head unit.
safety_warning: Do not try to fix screen issues while driving. Pull over safely.

symptom: High-pitched squeal when starting the car
beginner_steps: Open the hood and visually inspect the large rubber belt at the front of the engine. Look for cracks, fraying, or a shiny glazed surface.
mechanic_advice: If the belt is cracked or loose, a mechanic should replace it and check the tensioner pulley.
safety_warning: Keep hands, loose clothing, and hair far away from belts while the engine is running.

symptom: High engine RPMs but car accelerates very slowly
beginner_steps: Drive at a slow speed in a high gear and press the gas. If the engine revs up but the car doesn't speed up, the clutch is slipping.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic must separate the engine and transmission to install a new clutch kit.
safety_warning: A slipping clutch will eventually strand you; drive it straight to a shop.

symptom: Steering wheel suddenly becomes very heavy
possible_fault: Blown EPS fuse or faulty steering motor
beginner_steps: Check the fuse box for the EPS (Electronic Power Steering) fuse to see if it is blown. Restart the car to see if the system resets.
mechanic_advice: If the fuse is good, the steering rack or electric motor requires professional replacement.
safety_warning: It is incredibly difficult to steer a car safely without power assist at low speeds.

symptom: Bike smells like petrol and drips fuel from the bottom
beginner_steps: Look underneath the engine where the carburetor is located. Tap the side of the carburetor gently with the plastic handle of a screwdriver to unstick the float.
mechanic_advice: If it continues leaking, a mechanic must rebuild the carburetor and replace the rubber seals.
safety_warning: Leaking petrol is a severe fire hazard. Do not start the bike or park near open flames.

symptom: Oil leaking down the front suspension tubes
beginner_steps: Wipe the silver tubes of the front forks clean. Push down on the handlebars a few times. If a ring of oil appears on the tube, the seals are blown.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic needs to dismantle the front forks to replace the rubber seals and fork oil.
safety_warning: Leaking fork oil can drip onto your front brake pads, causing complete brake failure.

symptom: White smoke billowing from the engine area
beginner_steps: Turn off the bike immediately. Look for green or pink fluid leaking onto the engine or ground.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic must pressure test the system to find the burst hose or broken radiator.
safety_warning: Never open a motorcycle radiator cap when the engine is hot; boiling coolant will spray out.

symptom: Scooter engine revs loudly but rear wheel doesn't turn
beginner_steps: Put the scooter on its center stand. If you twist the throttle and hear the engine but feel no forward pull, the internal rubber belt has snapped.
mechanic_advice: A mechanic must remove the transmission cover and install a new drive belt and rollers.
safety_warning: Do not keep revving the engine if the belt is broken, as it can damage the pulleys.

symptom: Scooter stutters and dies when riding through rain
possible_fault: Water entering the spark plug boot
beginner_steps: Wait for the engine to cool. Pull the rubber cap off the spark plug and dry the inside of it thoroughly. Push it firmly back onto the plug.
mechanic_advice: If it happens every time it rains, a mechanic needs to install a new, waterproof spark plug cap.
safety_warning: Ensure the ignition is turned off before touching the spark plug wire.
